description: Comprehensive vocabulary crosswalk for E343 Math Methods at Indiana University. Maps equivalent or competing terms across six frameworks to reduce cognitive overload when students must coordinate vocabulary from Van de Walle, Five Practices (Smith & Stein), CDM/Talk Moves (Chapin), Indiana Academic Standards, CCSS, and Illustrative Mathematics.

confusion_risk_key.HIGH: Frameworks use the SAME word for DIFFERENT things, or DIFFERENT words for the SAME thing in ways likely to confuse students
confusion_risk_key.MEDIUM: Terms differ but the mapping is fairly transparent once explained
confusion_risk_key.LOW: Minor variation or only one framework addresses the concept

entries.concept: Addition problem where something is added to an initial quantity
entries.note: VdW uses 'Join' which sounds like combining (Part-Part-Whole) but specifically means an action of adding to an existing quantity. Indiana/CCSS/IM use 'Add to' which is more transparent. Students confuse Join with Part-Part-Whole.

entries.concept: Subtraction problem where something is removed
entries.note: VdW 'Separate' implies physical action but is less intuitive than 'Take from.' Students sometimes confuse 'Separate' with 'Compare' since both involve subtraction.

entries.concept: Two parts combine into a whole (or whole decomposed into parts)
entries.note: VdW emphasizes that Part-Part-Whole has NO ACTION, which is the key distinction from Join. Students confuse this with Join because both involve addition. The Indiana/CCSS phrasing 'Putting together' unfortunately implies action.

entries.concept: Two quantities compared to find the difference
entries.note: Fairly consistent across frameworks. Confusion arises because Compare problems can ask 'how many more' OR 'how many fewer' and students find the 'fewer' version much harder.

entries.concept: Division where the number of groups is known, find group size
entries.ccss: Number of objects in each share
entries.note: VdW and Indiana use 'partition' which is also used for fractions (partitioning a whole). CCSS describes the action rather than naming it. IM uses a question format. The word 'partition' doing double duty (division AND fractions) is a major source of confusion.

entries.concept: Division where group size is known, find number of groups
entries.ccss: Number of shares / equal shares of
entries.note: VdW calls this 'measurement division' which is confusing because 'measurement' also refers to rulers/units. Indiana does not name this type explicitly. IM uses a question format. Students struggle to see why this is called 'measurement.'

entries.concept: Knowing single-digit facts automatically
entries.note: VdW distinguishes 'automaticity' (quick recall) from 'fluency' (efficient + accurate + flexible + appropriate strategy use). Indiana conflates fluency and mastery. CCSS says 'know from memory.' IM uses 'fluency' broadly. The course PPTs explicitly state: 'Automaticity is different from fluency.'

entries.concept: Using strategies rather than rote recall for facts
entries.note: VdW places this in a three-phase developmental trajectory (counting, reasoning strategies, mastery). Indiana and CCSS list specific strategies. The risk is students thinking 'strategies' means tricks or shortcuts rather than understanding-based reasoning.

entries.concept: Multiplication with arrays and equal groups
entries.indiana: Equal-sized groups, arrays, area models, equal intervals on a number line
entries.note: All frameworks recognize these models but emphasize them differently. VdW treats them as distinct problem structures. Indiana lists them as equivalent representations. The key confusion is array vs. area model -- arrays show discrete objects while area models show continuous area.

entries.concept: Teacher orchestrates whole-class discussion of student work
entries.indiana: PS.3: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others
entries.cdm: Steps 3-4 talk moves in whole-class context
entries.ccss: MP.3: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others
entries.note: VdW calls this 'After phase,' IM calls it 'Synthesis' or 'Synthesize,' Five Practices breaks it into three sub-practices, CDM frames it as talk move steps. Students must map these onto each other when writing lesson plans.

entries.concept: Teacher circulates during student work time to understand thinking
entries.indiana: PS.1: Make sense of problems (teacher modeling this disposition)
entries.cdm: Using talk moves in small-group conversations
entries.note: Five Practices subdivides monitoring into tracking (who did what), assessing (questions that surface thinking), and advancing (questions that push thinking forward). VdW and IM treat this as a single phase. CDM provides the specific talk moves to use. Students must integrate all three frameworks when planning this part of a lesson.

entries.concept: Choosing tasks that require genuine mathematical thinking
entries.van_de_walle: Teaching through problem solving / Worthwhile tasks
entries.indiana: PS.1: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them
entries.five_practices: Practice 0: Setting Goals and Selecting Tasks
entries.ccss: MP.1: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them
entries.note: VdW frames this as a pedagogical stance ('teaching through problem solving'). Five Practices makes it a numbered practice (Practice 0). IM embeds high-demand tasks in the curriculum itself. The terminology overlap is manageable but students sometimes conflate 'selecting tasks' (Practice 0) with 'selecting student work' (Practice 3).

entries.concept: Three-phase lesson structure for problem-based instruction
entries.illustrative_math: Launch -> Work -> Synthesize (within: Warm Up -> Activities -> Synthesis -> Cool Down)
entries.note: Four different sets of names for essentially the same three phases. The course PPTs use 'Launch-Explore-Summarize' as the primary frame but require students to map Five Practices onto it. IM adds a four-part structure (Warm Up, Activities, Synthesis, Cool Down) nested within the three phases. The final exam explicitly asks students to know 'launch-explore-summarize' and when each of the 5 Practices occurs within it.

entries.concept: The quantity that a fraction refers to
entries.note: VdW uses 'referent unit' which is a technical term students rarely encounter elsewhere. Indiana/CCSS say 'the same whole' only in comparison contexts. IM alternates between 'the whole' and 'the unit.' The course PPTs emphasize: 'Fractions are relative -- the size of the whole makes a difference.'

entries.concept: Building fractions by repeating a unit fraction
entries.illustrative_math: Iterating / Copies of a unit fraction
entries.note: VdW and IM both use 'iterating' but VdW ties it to the 'measurement construct' of fractions. CCSS uses formal notation. Indiana uses 'iterations' in standard 3.NS.2. The course PPTs frame this as 'remove one share and repeat it.'

entries.concept: Teacher questions that surface current student thinking
entries.note: Five Practices names these 'assessing questions' which students confuse with assessment (tests/quizzes). CDM calls them 'probing questions' which overlaps with Steps 1-2 talk moves. The course calls these 'stay and listen questions' informally. Students must learn that 'assessing' here means understanding thinking, not grading.

entries.concept: Teacher questions that push student thinking forward
entries.cdm: Talk moves Steps 3-4 (press for reasoning, extend)
entries.note: Five Practices' 'advancing questions' are contrasted with 'assessing questions' -- a distinction students find hard to maintain. The course informally calls these 'walk away questions' because they give students something to work on. CDM's Steps 3-4 overlap but are not identical.

entries.concept: What students should understand vs. what students should be able to do
entries.van_de_walle: Understanding / Conceptual knowledge vs. Procedural fluency
entries.ccss: Standards for Mathematical Practice (understanding) + Content Standards (performance)
entries.note: The course PPTs devote an entire session to learning goals vs. performance goals. Key insight: 'If you can start it with Students will be able to... it is a performance goal (SWBAT).' Students can meet a performance goal WITHOUT meeting the related learning goal by following a procedure without understanding. The course notes: 'Your future school curriculum might call these ideas something different.'

entries.concept: Strategic teacher talk that structures productive mathematical discussion
entries.cdm: Talk Moves (Wait Time, Turn & Talk, Revoicing, Say More, Who Can Repeat, Press for Reasoning, Agree/Disagree, Who Can Add On)
entries.five_practices: Embedded in Monitoring and Connecting practices
entries.indiana: PS.3: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others
entries.ccss: MP.3: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others
entries.note: CDM provides the most specific and actionable vocabulary. Five Practices embeds discourse within its practices without naming specific moves. VdW treats it as a general pedagogical principle. Students must learn CDM's specific moves AND know where they fit in the Five Practices framework.

entries.concept: Assessment that reveals how students think, not just whether they are correct
entries.cdm: Talk Moves Steps 1-2 as assessment tools
entries.indiana: (Not named in standards; implied by PS.1)
entries.note: Each framework treats formative assessment differently. VdW and Five Practices emphasize in-the-moment assessment during lessons. IM has a specific 'cool-down' component that serves this purpose. CDM frames talk moves themselves as assessment tools.

entries.concept: One-on-one interview to understand a student's mathematical reasoning
entries.five_practices: Clinical interview / Related to anticipating and monitoring
entries.illustrative_math: (Not a curriculum component; used in research)
entries.note: The course calls this a Think-Aloud Interview (TAI). This is a course-specific term that maps to VdW's 'diagnostic interview' and the research tradition of 'clinical interviews.' Students conduct TAIs as a major assignment.

entries.concept: Progression from physical objects to drawings to symbols in student strategies
entries.five_practices: Sequencing from less to more sophisticated strategies
entries.note: VdW's CRA framework is well-known but the course PPTs use 'concrete, semi-concrete, and abstract' instead. IM embeds this progression in its activity design. Five Practices uses it as a sequencing rationale ('most concrete first'). The final exam asks about 'concrete, semi-concrete and abstract representations.'

entries.concept: Student engagement where struggle is productive rather than frustrating
entries.van_de_walle: Teaching through problem solving / Productive struggle
entries.five_practices: Created by setting appropriate goals + high-demand tasks
entries.indiana: PS.1: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them
entries.ccss: MP.1: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them
entries.illustrative_math: Maintaining cognitive demand during Work phase
entries.note: The concept is universal but named differently. VdW names it explicitly. Five Practices frames it as an outcome of good task selection. Indiana/CCSS embed it in 'persevere.' IM operationalizes it by maintaining cognitive demand and not giving away solutions in the Launch.

entries.concept: Repeating or restating a student's idea to make it public and check understanding
entries.note: CDM provides the specific term 'revoicing.' Other frameworks recognize the practice but don't name it. Students learn this as a CDM talk move and must recognize it when they see it in Five Practices or IM contexts.

entries.concept: Asking students to justify, prove, or explain their reasoning
entries.indiana: PS.3: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others
entries.ccss: MP.3: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others
entries.illustrative_math: Not named; embedded in teacher guidance
entries.note: CDM's 'Press for Reasoning' is a specific talk move. Five Practices frames this as advancing questions. Indiana/CCSS frame it as a mathematical practice standard. Students need to know that Press for Reasoning is a specific CDM term for what other frameworks describe more generally.

entries.concept: Fractions as equal parts of a whole
entries.indiana: 3.NS.2: Understand a unit fraction as the quantity formed by one part when a whole is partitioned
entries.ccss: 3.NF.A.1: A fraction 1/b as the quantity formed by 1 part when a whole is partitioned into b equal parts
entries.illustrative_math: Unit fractions / Partitioning shapes and wholes
entries.note: VdW identifies part-whole as ONE of five fraction constructs (part-whole, measurement, division, operator, ratio). Indiana/CCSS describe the action without naming the construct. Students must learn that partitioning is the first and simplest fraction meaning, used in grades 1-2.

entries.concept: Two fractions that name the same amount
entries.van_de_walle: Equivalent fractions / Conceptual methods for generating equivalence
entries.indiana: 4.NS.3: Explain equivalence of fractions and compare fractions
entries.ccss: 3.NF.A.3: Equivalent fractions / 4.NF.A.1: Explain fraction equivalence using visual models
entries.note: Terminology is consistent across frameworks. The confusion is conceptual, not terminological -- students (and their future students) struggle with WHY 2/4 = 1/2, not what to call it.

entries.concept: Four progressive steps of productive classroom talk
entries.cdm: Steps 1-4: Clarify/Share -> Orient to Others -> Deepen Reasoning -> Engage with Others' Reasoning
entries.note: CDM's four steps are the most detailed framework for classroom talk. They map onto but do not align one-to-one with Five Practices. Steps 1-2 correspond roughly to Monitoring; Steps 3-4 to Connecting. Students must hold both taxonomies in mind.

entries.concept: Teacher restates student idea to check understanding and make it public
entries.note: Duplicate of entry 23 -- revoicing. CDM is the only framework that names this specific move.

entries.concept: Classroom norms for respectful and equitable mathematical discussion
entries.cdm: Chapter 2: Classroom Talk Norms (respectful discourse + equitable participation)
entries.indiana: PS.3: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others
entries.ccss: MP.3: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others
entries.note: Each framework assumes norms exist but frames them differently. CDM devotes an entire chapter to norms. Five Practices treats them as prerequisites. VdW discusses 'mathematical community.' Indiana/CCSS embed norms in Practice Standard 3.

entries.concept: Five meanings or constructs of fractions
entries.indiana: Not named as constructs; individual meanings appear across grade-level standards
entries.ccss: Not named as constructs; individual meanings appear across domains
entries.illustrative_math: Not named as constructs; different meanings emerge across grade-level units
entries.note: VdW's five constructs framework is a major organizing idea in the course. No other framework names them. Students must learn these as a VdW-specific taxonomy and then recognize them implicitly in standards and IM lessons. The course PPTs and final exam explicitly test knowledge of these five constructs.

entries.concept: Fraction as a measure on the number line (iterating unit fractions)
entries.indiana: 3.NS.3: Model a non-unit fraction on a number line by marking equal lengths
entries.ccss: 3.NF.A.2: Understand a fraction as a number on the number line
entries.illustrative_math: Fractions on a number line / Iterating unit fractions
entries.note: VdW calls this the 'measurement construct' -- but 'measurement' also means rulers, length, area, volume. Indiana/CCSS describe the action (marking number lines). IM uses 'iterating.' The course PPTs define it as: 'make equal shares of a whole, remove one share, repeat the unit fraction.' This is the primary fraction meaning for 3rd grade.

entries.concept: Fraction as the result of dividing one whole number by another
entries.indiana: 5.NS.2: Explain different interpretations of fractions, including as parts of a whole, division of whole numbers, and parts of a set
entries.ccss: 5.NF.B.3: Interpret a fraction as division of the numerator by the denominator
entries.illustrative_math: Division situations that result in fractions
entries.note: VdW names this the 'division construct' as one of five fraction meanings. This is not introduced until 4th-5th grade. The course PPTs demonstrate this with 'sharing multiple equal bars among multiple people.' Students confuse this with partition division of whole numbers.

entries.concept: Fraction operating on (multiplying) another quantity
entries.indiana: 5.CA.5: Use visual fraction models to multiply a fraction by a fraction
entries.ccss: 5.NF.B.4: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication to multiply a fraction by a whole number
entries.illustrative_math: Fraction of a quantity / Fraction multiplication
entries.note: VdW names this the 'operator construct' -- taking 1/3 of 15 or 1/3 of 1/2. The course PPTs introduce this with set models (discrete objects). Indiana/CCSS describe it as multiplication. IM frames it as 'fraction of a quantity.' Students may not recognize that '1/3 of 15' IS multiplication.

entries.concept: Three visual models for representing fractions
entries.van_de_walle: Area model, Length model, Set model
entries.indiana: Visual fraction models (referenced across 3.NS through 5.CA standards)
entries.illustrative_math: Area diagrams, number lines, discrete diagrams
entries.note: VdW names three distinct visual models. Indiana/CCSS reference 'visual fraction models' without specifying which. IM uses 'area diagrams' (not 'area models'), 'number lines' (not 'length models'), and doesn't consistently name the set model. The course PPTs explicitly teach all three: 'Three kinds of visual models: Area, Length, Set.'

entries.concept: Equation that matches the story vs. equation that can be calculated
entries.note: The course PPTs introduce this explicitly: '__ + 25 = 40 is the semantic equation (follows the story); 40 - 25 = __ is the calculation equation (can be calculated this way).' VdW makes this distinction but no other framework does. Students struggle to see that the same problem can have both representations and that the semantic equation is important for algebraic reasoning.

entries.concept: The four components that make up fact fluency
entries.van_de_walle: Efficiency, Accuracy, Flexibility, Appropriate strategy selection
entries.note: VdW defines fluency as four specific components. Indiana, CCSS, and IM use 'fluency' without defining it, which students (and many teachers) interpret as 'fast recall.' The course PPTs explicitly teach: 'Efficiency, Accuracy, Flexibility, Appropriate strategy selection.' This is tested on the final exam.

entries.concept: Three developmental phases children go through in learning basic facts
entries.van_de_walle: Phase 1: Counting (counting all, counting on) -> Phase 2: Reasoning strategies (doubles, making 10) -> Phase 3: Mastery (automaticity)
entries.indiana: Not described as phases; standards list strategies
entries.ccss: Not described as phases; standards list strategies
entries.illustrative_math: Implicit in unit sequencing but not named as phases
entries.note: VdW's three-phase trajectory is a core course concept. The final exam tests it. No other framework names these phases. The critical point is that Phase 2 (strategies) is the road to Phase 3 (mastery) -- memorization that bypasses Phase 2 is counterproductive.

entries.concept: Three approaches to teaching basic facts
entries.van_de_walle: Memorization / Explicit Strategy Instruction / Guided Invention
entries.indiana: Not specified (standards say 'using strategies such as...')
entries.illustrative_math: Embedded in curriculum design (tends toward guided invention)
entries.note: VdW names three distinct approaches. The course PPTs emphasize that memorization 'often bypasses Phase 2' and that strategies are 'ways to think about problems, not tricks to be memorized.' Guided invention is the most aligned with Five Practices. Students must understand the difference between teaching a strategy and having students invent strategies.

entries.concept: Quick mental recall of basic facts without conscious strategy use
entries.note: VdW explicitly distinguishes automaticity from fluency: 'Automaticity means quick recall of basic facts. Fact fluency is the road that leads to automaticity.' Indiana conflates them. CCSS says 'know from memory.' This distinction is a major course theme and is tested on the final exam.

entries.concept: A short, daily routine where students mentally solve a computation and share strategies
entries.van_de_walle: Number Talk (referenced but not named as a VdW concept)
entries.illustrative_math: Warm-Up routines (Number Talks, Which One Doesn't Belong, True or False, etc.)
entries.five_practices: Not named; example of a task that can be orchestrated using all five practices
entries.cdm: Not named; exemplifies all four talk move steps
entries.note: Number Talks are a widely used routine taught in the course. IM includes them as one of several Warm-Up routines. Neither VdW nor Five Practices nor CDM 'own' the concept, but the course uses Number Talks as a vehicle to practice talk moves and demonstrate fact fluency teaching.

entries.concept: Five multiplicative problem structures
entries.van_de_walle: Equal Groups, Comparison, Array, Area, Combination (Cartesian product)
entries.illustrative_math: Equal groups, arrays, area diagrams, comparison
entries.note: VdW identifies FIVE distinct structures; Indiana/CCSS/IM list only three or four. Combination (Cartesian product) problems are rarely in standards. The course PPTs teach all five and require students to identify problem structure and unknown parts. Array vs. Area is confusing because arrays are rows/columns of discrete objects while area models use continuous regions.

entries.concept: Commutative property of multiplication
entries.van_de_walle: Commutative property (demonstrated with arrays: 3 rows of 5 = 5 rows of 3)
entries.indiana: 4.CA.3: Show how the order in which two numbers are multiplied (commutative property) does not change the product
entries.ccss: 3.OA.B.5: Apply properties of operations (commutative property of multiplication)
entries.note: The property itself is consistent across frameworks, but the grade placement differs: CCSS introduces it in 3rd grade, Indiana in 4th grade. The course PPTs use area models and arrays to demonstrate it: '10 groups of 4 equals 4 groups of 10.'

entries.concept: Distributive property of multiplication over addition
entries.indiana: 4.CA.3: Show how a multiplication problem can use the distributive property
entries.note: VdW and Indiana use the formal name 'distributive property.' IM and the course PPTs also call it 'break apart.' The course uses it as a key multiplication fact strategy: 6 x 7 = 6 x 5 + 6 x 2. Students confuse this with the 'add a group' strategy which is a special case. Grade placement differs: CCSS 3rd grade, Indiana 4th grade.

entries.concept: Strategies for multi-digit multiplication
entries.van_de_walle: Cluster problems / Number strings -> Area model (connected) -> Open array -> Partial products -> Standard algorithm
entries.indiana: 4.CA.1: Multiply using strategies based on place value and the properties of operations
entries.ccss: 4.NBT.B.5: Multiply using strategies based on place value and properties of operations
entries.illustrative_math: Area diagrams -> Partial products -> Standard algorithm
entries.five_practices: Example of sequencing from concrete to abstract
entries.note: VdW provides the most detailed progression: cluster problems -> concrete area model with base-ten blocks -> open array (semi-concrete) -> partial products -> standard algorithm. The course PPTs call the open array 'a semi-concrete representation of the area model.' IM uses 'area diagrams' not 'area models.' Students must understand that the standard algorithm IS partial products compressed into fewer lines.

entries.concept: Strategies for multi-digit division
entries.van_de_walle: Sharing division (base-ten blocks) / Measurement division (open arrays) -> Partial quotients -> Standard algorithm
entries.indiana: 4.CA.2: Find whole-number quotients and remainders with up to four-digit dividends and one-digit divisors
entries.ccss: 4.NBT.B.6: Find whole-number quotients and remainders using strategies based on place value
entries.note: VdW distinguishes between models for sharing division (base-ten blocks work well) and measurement division (open arrays work well). The course PPTs teach both explicitly. IM and standards don't make this distinction for multi-digit division. Students must learn that the division model affects which representation is most helpful.

entries.concept: Partial products as a multiplication strategy
entries.van_de_walle: Partial products (each sub-rectangle in an area model)
entries.illustrative_math: Partial products / Sub-rectangles in area diagrams
entries.note: The term 'partial products' is consistent but the visual representation varies. VdW connects partial products to area model sub-rectangles. The course PPTs show this connection explicitly: 'Each section is a partial product. Figure out the size of each sub-rectangle and combine to find the whole.'

entries.concept: Place value understanding for multi-digit computation
entries.van_de_walle: Composing and decomposing / Base-ten representations / Equivalent representations
entries.indiana: 1.NS through 3.NS: Place value across grades (tens, hundreds, thousands)
entries.ccss: 1.NBT through 4.NBT: Understand place value / Use place value understanding
entries.illustrative_math: Place value / Composing and decomposing by place value
entries.note: VdW uses 'equivalent representations' (e.g., 100 = 8 tens and 20 ones) which is a key concept for regrouping. Indiana uses 'number sense' strand. CCSS uses 'Numbers and Operations in Base Ten (NBT)' domain. IM uses 'composing and decomposing.' The course PPTs note: '2nd students work on understanding base ten and EQUIVALENT representations for three-digit numbers.' The concept of 'regrouping' vs. 'carrying/borrowing' is also a terminology issue.

entries.concept: Trading 10 ones for 1 ten (or vice versa) during computation
entries.indiana: Using number sense and place value strategies
entries.note: Older textbooks say 'carrying' and 'borrowing.' VdW uses 'regrouping' and 'trading.' Indiana avoids naming the action. CCSS uses 'compose/decompose.' The course goal examples reference 'when solving a two-digit addition problem with regrouping that ten ones creates one ten.' Students' future K-12 students will likely hear 'carry' and 'borrow' from parents.

entries.concept: The standard step-by-step procedure for an arithmetic operation
entries.van_de_walle: Standard algorithm (endpoint of a developmental progression)
entries.note: All frameworks use 'standard algorithm' but differ on when to introduce it and how. VdW treats it as the endpoint of a progression from concrete models through partial products. The course PPTs show the connection: standard algorithm IS partial products in compressed notation. The confusion is that students (and parents) think the algorithm IS the math, rather than one representation of it.

entries.concept: Using known facts and relationships to figure out unknown facts
entries.indiana: Strategies such as the relationship between multiplication and division (3.CA.5)
entries.ccss: Using strategies such as the relationship between multiplication and division (3.OA.C.7)
entries.illustrative_math: Using known products to find unknown products
entries.note: VdW names specific strategies: doubling, near doubles, making 10, pretend-a-10, using 5 as anchor. The course PPTs teach these explicitly. Indiana/CCSS list strategy types without naming them. IM embeds them in curriculum. The N101 prerequisite course uses different names for some of these strategies (e.g., 'chunking' instead of 'down under 10').

entries.concept: Specific addition fact strategies
entries.van_de_walle: One more/two more, Adding zero, Doubles, Near doubles, Combinations of 10, Making 10, Pretend-a-10 (Use 10), Using 5 as anchor
entries.note: VdW names eight specific strategies. The course PPTs teach all of them and note they differ from N101 prerequisite course names: 'Think addition = difference in N101; Down from 10 = chunking in N101; Take from 10 = decomposition in N101.' Students must coordinate VdW names, N101 names, and whatever names their field placement teachers use.

entries.concept: Specific multiplication fact strategies
entries.van_de_walle: Doubling, Add a group (distributive property), Break apart, Making bases from N101, Skip counting
entries.indiana: Strategies such as the relationship between multiplication and division (3.CA.5)
entries.illustrative_math: Doubling, using known facts, breaking apart factors
entries.note: VdW names specific strategies. The course PPTs demonstrate them with visual models. 'Add a group' is a special case of the distributive property. 'Making bases' comes from N101 and is not in VdW. Students must map between VdW strategy names and what they learned in N101.

entries.concept: Indiana Process Standards vs. CCSS Mathematical Practices
entries.ccss: MP.1 through MP.8 (8 Standards for Mathematical Practice)
entries.illustrative_math: MP.1 through MP.8 (uses CCSS Mathematical Practices directly)
entries.five_practices: Five Practices overlap with PS.1, PS.3, PS.4 but are NOT the same thing
entries.note: Indiana PS.1-8 are nearly identical to CCSS MP.1-8 in wording, but they are technically different documents. IM uses CCSS MPs. The CRITICAL confusion: Five Practices (Anticipate, Monitor, Select, Sequence, Connect) are TEACHER practices, not STUDENT practices, and are NOT the same as Process Standards / Mathematical Practices which describe what STUDENTS do. The course PPTs list all 8 Process Standards and have students create visuals for each.

entries.concept: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them
entries.van_de_walle: Teaching through problem solving / Productive struggle
entries.note: PS.1/MP.1 is the broadest practice standard. VdW's 'teaching through problem solving' creates the conditions for it. Five Practices supports it through task selection and monitoring. Students must understand this is a STUDENT practice, not a teaching technique.

entries.concept: Model with mathematics
entries.illustrative_math: MP.4 (students create representations of mathematical situations)
entries.five_practices: Multiple representations appear in sequencing rationale
entries.note: 'Model' is dangerously ambiguous. PS.4/MP.4 means students use math to represent real-world situations. VdW uses 'model' to mean physical manipulatives (base-ten blocks, fraction bars). The course PPTs use both meanings. A 'model' can be a physical object, a drawing, or a mathematical equation depending on context.

entries.concept: Teacher tracking which students used which strategies during work time
entries.note: Five Practices provides the most concrete tool: a monitoring chart where teachers record which students did which anticipated (or unanticipated) solutions. The course PPTs teach students to use monitoring charts and to plan for circulation patterns. VdW mentions observation but doesn't provide a specific tool.

entries.concept: Selecting which student work to share in whole-class discussion
entries.illustrative_math: Suggestions for selecting student work in teacher guides
entries.note: Five Practices' 'selecting' (Practice 3) is easily confused with 'selecting tasks' (Practice 0). The course PPTs address this: 'Selecting is deciding WHICH mathematical ideas (what) and students (who) the teacher will focus on during discussion.' It is purposeful, not random, and guided by learning goals.

entries.concept: Ordering shared student work to create a coherent mathematical storyline
entries.note: Five Practices provides specific sequencing rationales: most common strategy first, most concrete first, begin with misconception, put contrasting strategies back-to-back. The course PPTs warn: 'DON'T always use the same sequencing strategy -- kids will catch on.'

entries.concept: Making explicit links between different student solutions and to learning goals
entries.illustrative_math: Synthesis phase / Making connections across representations
entries.note: Five Practices identifies two types of connecting: connecting student work TO learning goals, and connecting student solutions TO EACH OTHER. The course PPTs teach both. IM's Synthesis phase serves this purpose. CDM's Steps 3-4 provide the talk moves to accomplish it.

entries.concept: Asking a student to elaborate on an unclear or brief statement
entries.illustrative_math: Not named; embedded in teacher guidance
entries.note: CDM names this 'Say More' -- one of the Step 1 talk moves. The course teaches it alongside Revoicing. Students practice it during TAI rehearsals.

entries.concept: Asking students to listen to and restate another student's idea
entries.illustrative_math: Not named; embedded in teacher guidance
entries.note: CDM names this 'Who Can Repeat?' -- a Step 2 talk move that helps students orient to the thinking of others. Easily confused with Revoicing (which is the TEACHER restating), not a student.

entries.concept: Asking students whether they agree or disagree with a claim and why
entries.cdm: Do You Agree or Disagree... Why? (Step 4 talk move)
entries.indiana: PS.3: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others
entries.ccss: MP.3: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others
entries.illustrative_math: Not named; embedded in synthesis discussions
entries.note: CDM names this as a Step 4 talk move. It directly supports PS.3/MP.3. The course PPTs teach it in Week 6.

entries.concept: Asking students to build on what another student said
entries.cdm: Who Can Add On? (Step 4 talk move)
entries.illustrative_math: Not named; embedded in teacher guidance
entries.indiana: PS.3: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others
entries.ccss: MP.3: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others
entries.note: CDM names this 'Who Can Add On?' -- a Step 4 talk move that helps students engage with the reasoning of others.

entries.concept: Wait time: silence after asking a question to allow thinking
entries.illustrative_math: Quiet Think Time (named in warm-up routines)
entries.note: CDM names 'Wait Time' as a foundational talk move. IM calls it 'Quiet Think Time.' The concept is the same but the names differ. Students may not realize these are the same practice.

entries.concept: Students discussing in pairs before sharing with the whole class
entries.cdm: Turn and Talk (foundational talk move / partner discussion)
entries.five_practices: Not named; can be used during any practice
entries.note: CDM calls this 'Turn and Talk.' IM and general education vocabulary use 'Think-Pair-Share.' They are essentially the same routine. Students encounter both terms and may not realize they refer to the same structure.

entries.concept: Teaching approach where teacher presents information to students
entries.five_practices: Contrasted with ambitious teaching / productive discourse
entries.cdm: Not addressed directly (CDM assumes inquiry-based context)
entries.illustrative_math: Not used; IM is designed for inquiry-based instruction
entries.note: The course PPTs contrast inquiry-based and lecture-based instruction: 'Inquiry: Students explore. Lecture: Teacher stands at front of class, talks to deliver information.' CDM distinguishes 'dialogic instruction' from 'direct instruction.' Neither is inherently wrong -- the course argues for inquiry as primary with some direct instruction for vocabulary and conventions.

entries.concept: Teaching approach where students explore problems before formal instruction
entries.van_de_walle: Teaching through problem solving / Inquiry-based instruction
entries.five_practices: Ambitious teaching / Orchestrating productive mathematical discussion
entries.note: Multiple overlapping terms for the same general approach. VdW says 'teaching through problem solving.' Five Practices says 'ambitious teaching.' CDM says 'dialogic instruction.' IM says 'problem-based curriculum.' The course PPTs note that 'every IM lesson CAN be taught as launch-explore-summarize and using 5 Practices, but they are not all written that way.' Students must understand these terms all point to the same pedagogical stance.

entries.concept: Purposeful selection of students to present, considering equity
entries.five_practices: Part of Selecting practice: 'Bias can affect ALL of us so it is critical to pay attention to race, gender, other background factors'
entries.cdm: Chapter 2: Equitable participation in classroom talk
entries.illustrative_math: Not addressed explicitly in curriculum; embedded in teacher guidance
entries.note: The course PPTs explicitly address equity in selecting: 'It is important for ALL students at different times to get a chance to share, NOT just the students with the clearest work or who are the most verbally articulate.' Five Practices and CDM both address this. Standards do not.

entries.concept: Cognitive demand of mathematical tasks
entries.five_practices: High-demand tasks (from Stein & Smith task analysis guide)
entries.note: Five Practices draws on the Stein & Smith task analysis guide which categorizes tasks by cognitive demand (memorization, procedures without connections, procedures with connections, doing mathematics). VdW uses 'worthwhile tasks.' The course PPTs emphasize: 'Students have to have something meaty to talk about.'

entries.concept: Launching a lesson: setting up the task without giving away the solution
entries.five_practices: Relates to Anticipating (Practice 1) -- prepared before launch
entries.note: VdW calls this 'Before,' CDM and the course use 'Launch,' IM has a specific 'Launch' section. The key tension: 'Maintain cognitive demand; Don't give away too much' vs. 'Make sure they have all the info and tools they need.' The course PPTs give detailed guidance: introduce vocabulary, provide tools and logistics, but don't model the solution.

entries.concept: Content strands in Indiana standards vs. CCSS domains
entries.van_de_walle: Chapters organized by topic (not by strand/domain)
entries.indiana: Number Sense (NS), Computation and Algebraic Thinking (CA), Geometry (G), Measurement (M), Data Analysis (DA)
entries.ccss: Counting & Cardinality (CC), Operations & Algebraic Thinking (OA), Number & Operations in Base Ten (NBT), Number & Operations -- Fractions (NF), Measurement & Data (MD), Geometry (G)
entries.illustrative_math: Units organized by mathematical topic, not by standard strand
entries.note: Indiana and CCSS organize content into different strands/domains with different names. Indiana combines 'Computation and Algebraic Thinking' into one strand; CCSS separates 'Operations & Algebraic Thinking' from 'Number & Operations in Base Ten.' Indiana has 'Number Sense'; CCSS splits this across multiple domains. Students reading standards must know which system they are looking at.

entries.concept: What Indiana calls a standard identification code
entries.van_de_walle: Referenced by topic/section description rather than code
entries.illustrative_math: References CCSS codes, not Indiana codes
entries.note: Indiana codes (3.CA.5) and CCSS codes (3.OA.C.7) look similar but use different abbreviations for different organizational structures. IM uses CCSS codes. The course requires students to identify appropriate Indiana standards for their lessons, but many resources reference CCSS codes. Students must be able to cross-reference both systems.

entries.concept: Vertical articulation: how content progresses across grade levels
entries.ccss: Progressions documents (Learning Progressions for each domain)
entries.illustrative_math: Scope and sequence / Unit narrative (explains progression)
entries.note: Indiana publishes 'vertical articulation' documents. CCSS has 'progressions' documents. IM has scope and sequence. VdW organizes chapters developmentally. The course requires students to analyze vertical articulation documents to understand what comes before and after their lesson.

entries.concept: Assessment built into daily instruction (not a separate test)
entries.van_de_walle: Formative assessment / Assessment through observation and questioning
entries.cdm: Talk moves as formative assessment tools
entries.note: The term 'formative assessment' is used broadly. VdW means observing and questioning during lessons. Five Practices means the assessing questions asked during monitoring. IM's Cool-down is a specific formative assessment artifact. CDM frames talk moves themselves as assessment. The course PPTs note that 'Timed tests do NOT help students LEARN basic facts' -- distinguishing formative from summative purposes.

entries.concept: End-of-lesson check for student understanding
entries.van_de_walle: Not named specifically (part of After phase)
entries.illustrative_math: Cool-down (specific section at end of each lesson)
entries.note: IM is the only framework that names this component. IM's 'Cool-down' serves as both a formative assessment and a consolidation of learning. Other frameworks assume some form of closure but don't name it. Students writing lesson plans may not include this unless they are using IM.

entries.concept: Tasks, problems, activities, and exercises as units of student work
entries.van_de_walle: Worthwhile tasks / Story problems / Contextualized problems / Exercises (for practice)
entries.five_practices: Tasks (the work students do, selected in Practice 0)
entries.cdm: Not distinguished (discusses how to talk about any mathematical work)
entries.illustrative_math: Activities (main sections of a lesson) / Problems (within activities) / Practice problems (homework)
entries.ccss: Problems (in standard language: 'Solve real-world and mathematical problems...')
entries.note: VdW distinguishes 'tasks' (requiring genuine thinking) from 'exercises' (practice of known procedures). Five Practices uses 'tasks' exclusively. IM uses 'activities' for lesson sections and 'problems' within them. Standards use 'problems.' The course PPTs frame the distinction: tasks require thinking; exercises are drill.

entries.concept: Area model as visual representation for multiplication vs. for fractions
entries.van_de_walle: Area model (multiplication: array of base-ten blocks; fractions: shaded region of a shape)
entries.indiana: Area models (3.CA.3 for multiplication; visual fraction models for fractions)
entries.note: 'Area model' is used in TWO completely different mathematical contexts in this course: (1) for multi-digit multiplication (connected rectangles showing partial products) and (2) for fractions (shaded portions of shapes showing fractional amounts). Students must understand these are the same NAME for different visual tools used for different purposes.

entries.concept: Open array / Open number line as a semi-concrete representation
entries.van_de_walle: Open array (for multiplication/division) / Open number line (for addition/subtraction)
entries.indiana: Not named in standards (strategies based on place value)
entries.illustrative_math: Open array (for multiplication/division) / Number line diagrams
entries.five_practices: Example of a representation between concrete and abstract
entries.note: The course PPTs call the open array 'a semi-concrete representation of the area model.' It is drawn as a blank rectangle (not to scale) subdivided by place value, with partial products recorded inside. Students must understand the progression: base-ten blocks -> connected array -> open array -> standard algorithm.
